corpname.part: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

descriptivenote.p: Primarily in English, with some material in German, Hungarian, and Russian.

did.abstract: The collection contains photographs, artwork, correspondence, printed material, and audiovisual and computer media documenting experimental photography and underground art in Eastern Europe collected by American editor, publisher, and curator John P. Jacob.

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: This collection is open for research.
Boxes 131-132 (audiovisual material): Restricted fragile material. Reference copies may be requested. Consult Access Services for further information.
Box 133 (computer media): Restricted fragile material. Access copies of digital files may be requested. Consult Access Services for further information.
Folder 1888 in Box 131 is unused.

acqinfo.head: Immediate Source of Acquisition
acqinfo.p: Purchased from John P. Jacob on the Edwin J. Beinecke Book Fund, 2018.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Organized into six series: I. Eastern European Photographs and Art, 1928-2005. II. Artwork by John P. Jacob, 1979-1988. III. Riding Beggar Press, 1948-2017. IV. Mail Art, 1973-1991. V. Printed Material, 1977-2018. VI. Audiovisual and Computer Media, 1980s-2015.

bioghist.head: John P. Jacob (1957-)
bioghist.p: John P. Jacob is an American artist, writer, publisher, photographer, and curator of avant-garde photography. He began his career as an artist working with reproductive media such as photography, rubber stamps, mail art, and artists' books. During the early 1980s, Jacob founded Riding Beggar Press and published the mail art zine PostHype and the International Portfolio of Artists' Photography (1983-1986), an assembling book project integrating photography, mail art, and book art. His interests in censorship and working with artists from the communist states of Eastern Europe inspired Jacob to travel to Czechoslovakia, East Germany, Hungary, and Poland in the mid-1980s, where he cultivated access to underground circles involved in experimental photography, mail art, and performance art. Jacob also collected photographs by those artists and curated exhibitions of their work in Eastern Europe – the first such transnational exhibits in the Eastern Bloc – and the United States. These exhibitions included "Out of Eastern Europe: Private Photographs," which premiered at the MIT List Visual Arts Center in 1987 before traveling to the Grey Art Gallery at New York University and other American galleries. In 1993, Jacob was appointed Executive Director of the Photographic Resource Center at Boston University. In 2003, he was appointed Founding Director of the Inge Morath Foundation, and from 2011 to 2015 he served as Program Director for the Magnum Foundation's Legacy Program. As of 2022, Jacob is McEvoy Family Curator for Photography at the Smithsonian American Art Museum.

prefercite.head: Preferred Citation
prefercite.p: John P. Jacob Collection. General Collection, Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library, Yale University.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The collection contains photographs, artwork, correspondence, printed material, and audiovisual and computer media documenting experimental photography and underground art in Eastern Europe collected by American editor, publisher, and curator John P. Jacob. The bulk of material consists of photographs by artists from East Germany, Poland, Hungary, and Czechoslovakia including Ágnes Eperjesi, Tibor Hajas, László Heged?s, Gábor Kerekes, Milan Kní?ák, Matthias Leupold, Géza Perneczky, Karla Sachse, Lenke Szilágyi, Tibor Várnagy, János Vet?, and Krystyna Ziach. Images depict everyday life and work in the Eastern Bloc, as well as objects, architecture, and nature. Also included are files related to proposed exhibitions or publications; letters to Jacob from colleagues and friends; artists' books and zines; mixed media, sculpture, lithographs, and other artwork; and offset and silkscreen posters. The collection additionally contains a small amount of photographs by Jacob; Riding Beggar Press project and publication files, including rubber stamps used by Jacob and materials related to PostHype and the International Portfolio of Artists' Photography ; and mail art by artists such as Anna Banana, Ryosuke Cohen, and Ray Johnson.

userestrict.head: Conditions Governing Use
userestrict.p: The John P. Jacob Collection is the physical property of the Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library, Yale University. Literary rights, including copyright, belong to the authors or their legal heirs and assigns. For further information, consult the appropriate curator.

processinfo.head: Processing Information
processinfo.p: Collections are processed to a variety of levels, depending on the work necessary to make them usable, their perceived research value, the availability of staff, competing priorities, and whether or not further accruals are expected. The library attempts to provide a basic level of preservation and access for all collections, and does more extensive processing of higher priority collections as time and resources permit.
This collection received a basic level of processing, including rehousing and minimal organization.

Information included in the Description of Papers note and Collection Contents section is drawn from information supplied with the collection and from an initial survey of the contents. Folder titles appearing in the contents list below are often based on those provided by the creator or previous custodian. Titles have not been verified against the contents of the folders in all cases. Otherwise, folder titles are supplied by staff during initial processing.
This finding aid may be updated periodically to account for new acquisitions to the collection and/or revisions in arrangement and description.
Folders 1297-1305 and 1866 are not in use.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This series consists of photographs, artists' books and zines, writings, artwork, posters, and correspondence. Included are materials John P. Jacob collected or solicited for exhibitions and publications, some of which were never realized, such as "Out of Eastern Europe: Private Photography," Second International Portfolio of Artists' Photography, "(VIEWS): Photography from East Germany," and "East German Artists Working in the Tradition of John Heartfield."

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Organized into nine subseries: Eastern European Photographs; Proposed Anthology: "Out of Eastern Europe: Private Photography"; Second Portfolio Project: Second International Portfolio of Artists' Photography ; Proposed Publication: "(VIEWS): Photography from East Germany"; Eastern European and Soviet Artists Books and Zines; Proposed Exhibition: "East German Artists Working in the Tradition of John Heartfield"; Other Artworks; Posters; and Eastern European Correspondence.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This subseries contains gelatin silver and Cibachrome prints, offset prints, mixed media, and collages collected by Jacob from artists active in Eastern Europe and the Soviet Union in the late 1970s through the mid-1990s. Artists from the German Democratic Republic include Tina Bara, Manfred Butzmann, Guillermo Deisler, Klaus Elle, Thomas Florschuetz, Joseph Huber, Matthias Leupold, Andreas Munstermann, Karla Sachse, Gundula Schulze el Dowy, and Maria Sewcz. Hungarian artists include Ágnes Eperjesi, Tibor Hajas, László 2. Hegedűs, Anna Karpati, Gábor Kerekes, László Lugosi Lugo, Lenke Szilágyi, Tibor Várnagy, and János Vető. Polish artists include Anna Beata Bohdziewicz, Jerzy Caryk, Kuba Pajewski, Josef Robakowski, and Krystyna Ziach. Artists from the USSR and Latvia include Frant︠s︡isko Infantė, Igor' Makarevich, and October Group. Czech artists include Karel Bartoníček, Milan Knížák, Libor Krejcar, and Miloš Šejn. The subseries also contains photographs of Jacob by or with other artists such as Bohdziewicz, István Halas, Adam Rzepecki, and Várnagy in Budapest the late 1980s.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Materials maintain order found at time of acquisition.

I would like them to be exposed in one sequence but if there are any difficulties they may be presented in a different way. I would be very much obliged if you could confirm receiving this postage. hoping to hear from you soon, I remain, Yours sincerely."

Proposed Anthology: "Out of Eastern Europe: Private Photography"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This subseries consists of essays and photographs, primarily gelatin silver and chromogenic prints, as well as a few transparencies and offset postcards, for an anthology developed for publication by MIT Press after the opening of the exhibition "Out of Eastern Europe: Private Photography." The book was never published.

Second Portfolio Project: Second International Portfolio of Artists' Photography

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This subseries consists of photographs solicited and assembled by Jacob in 1984-1985 for the Second International Portfolio of Artists' Photography. The project culminated in the 1986 exhibition "2nd International Portfolio," curated by Jacob and Tibor Várnagy, at Liget Galéria in Budapest. Photographs are mounted to paper folios with handwritten captions by Jacob.

Proposed Publication: "(VIEWS): Photography from East Germany"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This subseries consists of materials for an unpublished book of East German photography, including draft essays by Wolfgang Kil, Andreas Krase, Karla Sachse, and Gundula Schulze Eldowy, along with English translations of the works, and gelatin silver prints for reproduction by Schulze, Harald Hauswald, Ute Mahler, Evelyn Richter, and Thomas Sandberg, among others.

Eastern European and Soviet Artists Books and Zines

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This subseries contains limited editions and self-published books and magazines, artwork, posters, and ephemera from the Czech Republic, German Democratic Republic, Hungary, Poland, Romania, and the Soviet Union. Included are a number of issues of CommonPress, and works by György Galántai, Group Inconnu, Géza Perneczky, Tibor Várnagy, and others, as well as several collaborations of Karla Sachse with Chilean-born poet Guillermo Deisler and Ugandan author Varsha Nair.

Full exhibition/document/book in folio case, including: (A) 12 folded sheets housed in a folio, each stamped, embossed, and numbered 14/20; (B) text document including names of participating artists staple bound in red construction paper cover; (C) 96 individual pages with artwork mounted on cardstock, each identified by a typed paper label, stamp, embossed seal, and edition number.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This subseries contains works of art and other material related to a proposed exhibition organized by Jacob with assistance from Karla Sachse and Joseph Huber in the early 1990s. Included are offset, silkscreen, and gelatin silver prints by Manfred Butzmann, Martin Hoffman, Wolfram Jänisch, and Andreas Prüstel, among others. Many items are signed or annotated.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Series of twelve oversize envelopes bound at the left edge with four bolts, each envelope titled and numbered, and containing printed matter. Bound envelopes: #1 Kolkhoz, #2 Letters to the USSR, #3 Post Office Dinner, #4 Wonderkids, #5 Five Year Plan, #6 Stalin Test, 1981-1986. The first envelope signed and annotated in Russian and numbered 27/100 in pencil, and bearing a red wax seal on the verso.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Limited edition boxed set with hard cover book and folio of six silkscreen prints, together with the softcover book Samizdatu, published by Centrum Sztuki Wspolczesnej, Waesaw, 1990. Cover design by Valeriy Gerlovin, edition printed by Nenad Bozic. Willis Locker & Owens Publishing, NY: (1) Vagrich Bakhchani͡an, Flag, signed and dated 1986 in pencil; (2) Mikhail Chernyshov, Turret; (3) Rimma Gerlovina, Flat Cube, signed in pencil; (4) Valeriy Gerlovin, Out of the Wall, signed and dated 1986 in pencil; (5) Henry Khudyakov, The Big Cig, signed and dated 1986 in pencil; (6) Komar and Melamid, Venice from the Book Text, signed and dated 1986 in pencil. Five of six prints signed and numbered 16/125.

scopecontent.p: This subseries contains correspondence between Jacob and Eastern European artists, friends, and colleagues regarding exhibitions, publications, and other professional matters. scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This series consists of correspondence, photographs, slides, mail art, notes, publications, and financial records by or relating to Riding Beggar Press. Also included are some personal papers of John P. Jacob, such as passports.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Organized into two subseries: Projects and Correspondence; and Publications.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This subseries contains project files and correspondence relating to the mail art exhibitions "Censorship East/Censorship West" and "The Catalog of Ideas," and magazines and books such as PostHype, Howling Mad Mail, and the International Portfolio of Artists' Photography. Included are proposals, photographs, paste-ups, clippings, notes, and a box of rubber stamps used by John P. Jacob.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Materials maintain order found at time of acquisition.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Contains: an international group of mailing envelopes of various sizes, most hand-addressed to Jacob and Hype World HQ, some handmade, many with various stamps, stickers, and hand drawn decorative borders; announcements; some correspondence, brochures, and photocopies of articles; eight chromogenic prints by Larry Rippel entitled "Skeleton in A Flat Opus 12"; original collage by Janos Urban; additional original paintings, drawings and collages (unidentified).
